content.text: User Feedback Collection for GenAI Products

GenAI products present a unique feedback challenge. In a traditional software product, a bug is binary: the button either submits the form or it does not. In a GenAI product, quality is a spectrum: the response might be factually accurate but poorly phrased, or helpful to 90% of users but harmful to 5%, or excellent for coding tasks but mediocre for creative writing. Collecting feedback that captures this complexity requires a multi-signal approach that combines explicit user ratings, implicit behavioral signals, and structured evaluation programs.
This document covers how to collect each type of signal, the implementation details, privacy constraints, and how to use the combined signal to drive product improvement.

attrs.alt: User feedback signal types: explicit feedback (thumbs, correction, safety reports), implicit behavioral signals (copy rate, reformulation, session depth), and structured collection (human eval panels, A/B preference, RLHF annotation)

content.text: Explicit Feedback Mechanisms
Per-Response Rating

The thumbs up / thumbs down rating widget is the most widely deployed feedback mechanism in GenAI products. Its low friction — one click — maximizes response rate, which is necessary because most users do not provide any explicit feedback without prompting. The typical rating rate for a well-placed widget is 3–8% of responses.
Implementation principles that affect rating quality:
Show the widget immediately after the response completes
, not after a delay. Users who move on and then see the prompt are less likely to rate accurately.
Make thumbs down non-punitive
: users who fear that their rating will be used against the model or against them will under-rate. Frame it as 'help us improve' not 'report a problem'.
For thumbs down, show a category selector
: accuracy, helpfulness, safety, formatting. The category converts a binary signal into actionable data.
Store the rating with the full context
: the prompt, the response, the conversation history, the model version, and the timestamp. A rating without context cannot be used to improve the model.

# app/feedback/rating.py
# Handles per-response rating submission with full context storage

from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Optional
import hashlib, json, boto3

@dataclass
class ResponseRating:
    session_id: str
    response_id: str
    rating: str                          # "thumbs_up" | "thumbs_down"
    category: Optional[str] = None       # "accuracy" | "helpfulness" | "safety" | "formatting"
    comment: Optional[str] = None        # Free-text from user
    model_version: str = ""
    timestamp: str = ""

    # Context fields — stored for training signal; privacy-gated
    prompt_hash: str = ""                # SHA-256 of prompt (not the prompt itself)
    response_length: int = 0             # Character count (not the response itself)
    conversation_turn: int = 0

def save_rating(rating: ResponseRating, prompt: str, response: str,
                consent_level: str = "none") -> None:
    s3 = boto3.client("s3")
    data = asdict(rating)
    data["timestamp"] = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
    data["prompt_hash"] = hashlib.sha256(prompt.encode()).hexdigest()
    data["response_length"] = len(response)

    # Only store raw prompt/response if user has consented to training data use
    if consent_level == "full":
        data["prompt"] = prompt
        data["response"] = response
    elif consent_level == "anonymized":
        # Store prompt/response with PII removed (requires additional PII scrubbing pipeline)
        data["prompt"] = "[anonymized]"
        data["response"] = response  # responses typically do not contain PII
    # If consent_level == "none": only store hash and metadata (default)

    key = f"ratings/{rating.model_version}/{rating.session_id}/{rating.response_id}.json"
    s3.put_object(
        Bucket="feedback-store-prod",
        Key=key,
        Body=json.dumps(data),
        ServerSideEncryption="AES256",
    )
Free-Text Correction

The most valuable explicit feedback is a user correction: the user provides what the response should have said. This converts a negative rating into a labeled training example. The conversion rate is low (typically < 1% of negative ratings result in a correction), but the signal is high quality.

User corrections must be treated carefully for training use: they are the user's opinion of the correct answer, which may itself be incorrect. Use corrections to identify topics where the model consistently fails; do not use individual corrections as ground truth for fine-tuning without expert review.
Safety Reports
Safety reports are the highest-priority feedback signal. A user who reports a response as harmful, offensive, or policy-violating is providing a signal that must be reviewed regardless of volume.

# app/feedback/safety_report.py
# Handles user safety reports with immediate triage routing

from enum import Enum
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

class SafetyCategory(Enum):
    HARMFUL_CONTENT = "harmful_content"
    PRIVACY_VIOLATION = "privacy_violation"
    BIAS_OR_DISCRIMINATION = "bias_or_discrimination"
    MISINFORMATION = "misinformation"
    ILLEGAL_CONTENT = "illegal_content"
    OTHER = "other"

@dataclass
class SafetyReport:
    response_id: str
    session_id: str
    category: SafetyCategory
    user_comment: Optional[str]
    severity: str  # "critical" | "high" | "medium" | "low" — user-selected

def submit_safety_report(report: SafetyReport, response_content: str) -> None:
    # All safety reports are stored in a separate high-priority queue
    # and reviewed by the safety team within 24h (critical) or 72h (others)
    import boto3, json
    from datetime import datetime, timezone

    sqs = boto3.client("sqs")
    payload = {
        "type": "safety_report",
        "response_id": report.response_id,
        "category": report.category.value,
        "severity": report.severity,
        "user_comment": report.user_comment,
        "response_content": response_content,  # Always stored for safety review
        "timestamp": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
    }

    queue_url = {
        "critical": "https://sqs.../critical-safety-reports",
        "high": "https://sqs.../high-safety-reports",
    }.get(report.severity, "https://sqs.../safety-reports")

    sqs.send_message(QueueUrl=queue_url, MessageBody=json.dumps(payload))

    # Critical reports also page on-call safety engineer immediately
    if report.severity == "critical":
        import requests, os
        requests.post(os.environ["PAGERDUTY_EVENTS_URL"], json={
            "routing_key": os.environ["SAFETY_ONCALL_KEY"],
            "event_action": "trigger",
            "payload": {
                "summary": f"Critical safety report: {report.category.value}",
                "severity": "critical",
                "source": "user-safety-report",
            }
        })
Implicit Behavioral Signals

Implicit signals are inferred from user behavior without requiring any action from the user. Because they require no friction, they are available for nearly every interaction — unlike explicit ratings, which are only provided for a small fraction of responses.
Copy and Use Signal

When a user copies a response to clipboard, pastes it into another application, or executes code that the model generated, that action signals that the response was useful. The copy rate is one of the strongest behavioral proxies for response quality.
Session Continuation and Abandonment

In a chat interface, a user who asks a follow-up question is engaging productively. A user who closes the session or session-idles after a response is abandoning the interaction — which may indicate the response was unhelpful or confusing.

Session continuation rate is calculated as the fraction of turns that are followed by at least one more turn within a defined time window (typically 2 minutes for chat, 10 seconds for code completion). It is a leading indicator of user satisfaction and is more sensitive to quality changes than explicit rating, which lags by days.
Prompt Reformulation

When a user rephrases a question they already asked in the same session, that pattern signals that the previous response did not answer their actual question. Detecting reformulation requires semantic comparison of consecutive turns, not just lexical matching:

# feedback/reformulation_detector.py
# Detects when a user is reformulating a previous question

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

def cosine_similarity(a: np.ndarray, b: np.ndarray) -> float:
    return float(np.dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b)))

def detect_reformulation(turn_n: str, turn_n_minus_2: str,
                         threshold: float = 0.85) -> bool:
    # Compare turn N (new user input) with turn N-2 (previous user input)
    # High similarity suggests the user is re-asking the same question
    embeddings = model.encode([turn_n, turn_n_minus_2])
    sim = cosine_similarity(embeddings[0], embeddings[1])
    return sim >= threshold

# Example usage in the conversation pipeline
def process_turn(session: dict, new_user_input: str) -> dict:
    turns = session.get("turns", [])
    if len(turns) >= 2:
        # The turn 2 positions back is the previous user input (skipping model response)
        prev_user_inputs = [t["content"] for t in turns if t["role"] == "user"]
        if prev_user_inputs and detect_reformulation(new_user_input, prev_user_inputs[-1]):
            session["reformulation_detected"] = True
            # Log this as an implicit quality signal for the previous response
            log_implicit_signal(
                response_id=turns[-1]["id"],  # The response that was just reformulated
                signal_type="reformulation",
                context={"similarity": "high"},
            )
    return session
Structured Evaluation Programs

Explicit ratings and implicit signals measure user satisfaction with the product as deployed. Structured evaluation programs measure quality in a controlled setting, enabling comparison across model versions and fine-tuning experiments.
Human Evaluation Panels

A human evaluation panel is a group of trained annotators who rate model outputs against a defined rubric. The panel produces the ground-truth quality signal that automated metrics are calibrated against.
Evaluation Dimension
Rating Scale
What It Measures
Factual accuracy
1-5 (5 = fully accurate)
Does the response contain correct information?
Instruction following
1-5 (5 = fully followed)
Did the model do what the user asked?
Helpfulness
1-5 (5 = maximally helpful)
Did the response serve the user's actual goal?
Harmlessness
Binary + severity
Does the response contain harmful content?
Conciseness
1-5 (5 = appropriately concise)
Is the response an appropriate length for the question?

Human evaluation panels are expensive — a thorough evaluation of 500 responses across 5 dimensions costs 2-3 weeks of annotator time. This is why they are used to calibrate automated metrics, not to measure every release. A well-calibrated LLM-as-judge evaluator can rate 10,000 responses overnight at a fraction of the cost.
Preference Comparison (A/B Annotation)

Preference comparison presents annotators with two responses to the same prompt and asks which is better. This is the feedback format used to train reward models in RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback). It produces a relative ordering rather than an absolute score, which can be more reliable when the absolute quality scale is ambiguous.

# evaluation/preference_collection.py
# Records pairwise preference comparisons between two model responses

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal

@dataclass
class PreferenceAnnotation:
    prompt_id: str
    response_a_id: str
    response_b_id: str
    preferred: Literal["A", "B", "tie", "both_bad"]
    annotator_id: str
    confidence: Literal["clear", "slight", "uncertain"]
    dimensions: dict[str, Literal["A", "B", "tie"]]  # per-dimension preference

def compute_elo_ratings(annotations: list[PreferenceAnnotation],
                        initial_rating: float = 1000.0) -> dict[str, float]:
    ratings: dict[str, float] = {}
    K = 32  # ELO K-factor

    for ann in annotations:
        if ann.preferred in ("both_bad", "tie"):
            continue  # No Elo update for ties or bad pairs

        winner = ann.response_a_id if ann.preferred == "A" else ann.response_b_id
        loser  = ann.response_b_id if ann.preferred == "A" else ann.response_a_id

        ra = ratings.get(winner, initial_rating)
        rb = ratings.get(loser,  initial_rating)

        expected_a = 1.0 / (1 + 10 ** ((rb - ra) / 400))
        ratings[winner] = ra + K * (1 - expected_a)
        ratings[loser]  = rb + K * (0 - (1 - expected_a))

    return ratings
LLM-as-Judge Evaluation

LLM-as-judge uses a capable model (typically GPT-4o or Claude) to evaluate the quality of responses from the production model. It scales to large response volumes that human annotation cannot cover and can be run on every model update before deployment.

# evaluation/llm_judge.py
# Uses a judge LLM to evaluate response quality on a structured rubric

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

JUDGE_PROMPT = '''You are evaluating a response from a GenAI assistant.
Rate the response on the following dimensions, each on a 1-5 scale:

**Prompt**: {prompt}

**Response to evaluate**: {response}

**Rating dimensions**:
1. Factual accuracy (1=contains errors, 5=fully accurate)
2. Instruction following (1=ignored instructions, 5=fully followed)
3. Helpfulness (1=unhelpful, 5=maximally helpful)
4. Conciseness (1=too long/short, 5=appropriate length)

Output JSON only:
{{"factual_accuracy": N, "instruction_following": N, "helpfulness": N, "conciseness": N,
  "overall": N, "rationale": "one sentence explanation"}}'''

def evaluate_response(prompt: str, response: str) -> dict:
    result = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-5",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": JUDGE_PROMPT.format(prompt=prompt, response=response)
        }]
    )
    import json, re
    # Extract JSON from the response
    text = result.content[0].text
    json_match = re.search(r'\{.*\}', text, re.DOTALL)
    if json_match:
        return json.loads(json_match.group())
    raise ValueError(f"Could not parse judge response: {text}")

def batch_evaluate(prompt_response_pairs: list[tuple[str, str]]) -> list[dict]:
    return [evaluate_response(p, r) for p, r in prompt_response_pairs]

LLM-as-judge has known biases: it tends to prefer verbose responses, responses that match the judge's own style, and responses from the same model family. These biases must be calibrated against human evaluation panels. Use the judge's scores as a relative metric (this version of the model scores higher than the previous version) rather than an absolute quality measure.
Privacy Constraints on Feedback Data

User feedback data is uniquely sensitive for GenAI products. Prompts often contain personal information that users did not intend to be retained for training or analysis purposes. The feedback collection architecture must respect the privacy policy under which the inference was performed.
Consent Levels
Consent Level
What Can Be Stored
Typical Use Case
No consent
Rating (thumbs up/down), metadata (response length, model version, timestamp)
Default for most consumer products; feedback data used for aggregate analysis only
Anonymized consent
Rating + anonymized prompt/response (PII removed), behavioral signals
Products where users accept a data use agreement
Full training consent
Rating + full prompt + response; may be used as training data
Professional tools where users explicitly opt in to model improvement
Never store prompt content for training purposes without explicit user consent.

Even storing prompt hashes (SHA-256) may be subject to data protection law in some jurisdictions if the prompts could be reconstructed via rainbow table attacks. Consult legal counsel before designing the feedback data storage architecture.
Using Feedback to Drive Improvement
Collected feedback has value only if it drives product change. The signal-to-action pipeline defines how each type of feedback is routed to the team that can act on it:
Signal
Owner
Review Cadence
Potential Action
Safety reports (critical/high)
Safety team
Within 24h
Model rollback, safety filter update, policy change
Thumbs down with 'accuracy' category > threshold
ML team
Weekly
Identify failure mode; add to golden eval set; fine-tuning data collection
High reformulation rate on specific topic cluster
Product + ML team
Weekly
Identify instruction-following gap; prompt engineering or fine-tuning
LLM-as-judge score regression vs. prior version
ML team
Per release
Block deployment or fix regression before releasing
User interview qualitative themes
Product team
Monthly
Roadmap prioritization; feature design
Summary

User feedback collection for GenAI products requires a multi-signal approach because no single signal captures the full quality picture. Explicit ratings (thumbs, categories, corrections) provide labeled quality signals but at low volume. Implicit behavioral signals (copy rate, session continuation, reformulation) provide high-volume quality proxies without user friction. Structured evaluation programs (human panels, preference annotation, LLM-as-judge) provide calibrated quality measurements that enable comparison across model versions.

The architecture must respect the privacy policy under which inference was performed: storing prompt content for analysis or training requires explicit user consent. The default posture should store only metadata — ratings, behavioral event types, model version, timestamps — and store prompt content only when users have affirmatively opted in to model improvement.

For each type of feedback signal, the value depends on the signal-to-action pipeline that determines what happens with it. Safety reports that are not reviewed within 24 hours, thumbs-down ratings that never reach the ML team, and qualitative interview themes that never reach the product roadmap are signals without value. The feedback collection system and the feedback review process must be designed together.
